g.data-narration: This lesson covers Deterministic Pushdown Automata, an extension of finite automata with stack memory.
A DPDA extends finite automata with a stack that stores symbols, enabling it to recognize context-free languages.
g.text: A Deterministic Pushdown Automata extends finite automata with a stack memory.
It recognizes deterministic context-free languages - a proper subset of CFLs.
At most ONE transition for any (state, input, stack-top) combination.

g.data-narration: DPDAs are crucial in compiler design for LL and LR parsers, enabling efficient parsing without backtracking.
g.text: DPDA: At most ONE move from any config

g.data-narration: A DPDA is formally defined as a 7-tuple: states, input alphabet, stack alphabet, transition function, start state, initial stack symbol, and final states.
g.text: Formal Definition: 7-Tuple M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, Z₀, F)

g.data-narration: The configuration or instantaneous description of a DPDA is a triple: current state, remaining input, and stack contents.
DPDAs can perform three types of transitions: push symbols onto the stack, pop symbols from the stack, or replace the top symbol.
For a PDA to be deterministic, it must satisfy three conditions that ensure at most one move is possible from any configuration.
g.text: At most ONE: δ(q,a,X) or δ(q,ε,X)

g.data-narration: DPDAs can accept by final state or by empty stack, but these are NOT equivalent for deterministic PDAs, unlike NPDAs.
g.text: (q₀, w, Z₀) ⊢* (q, ε, γ), q ∈ F
Reach final state after consuming input
Important: For DPDAs, these are NOT equivalent!
DCFL(by final state) ⊂ DCFL(by empty stack)

g.data-narration: Example: The language 0^n1^n is a classic DPDA example. Push 0s onto the stack, then pop them for each 1 read.
Example: Even-length palindromes can be recognized by pushing input symbols, then switching state to pop and match in reverse.
Example: Balanced parentheses are recognized by pushing opening brackets and popping for each closing bracket to ensure matching.
DCFLs are closed under complement and quotient with regular languages, but NOT under union or intersection.
Key points: DPDAs extend finite automata with stack memory, enabling recognition of deterministic context-free languages used in parsing.
g.text: 2. Configuration = (q, w, γ) - state, input, stack
3. Push/Pop/Replace operations enable counting and matching
4. Final state ≠ Empty stack acceptance for DPDAs
5. DCFLs: Closed under complement, not under union
Used in Compiler Design - LL(k) and LR(k) Parsing
